Eren stopped the vehicle at a checkpoint that was not a checkpoint — two posts driven into cracked pavement, a chain between them, a sign written in three languages of which Corin could read only one. The readable portion said: *BEYOND THIS POINT, ARCHIVE GOVERNANCE DOES NOT APPLY. ENTER AT YOUR OWN RISK. THE UNWOVEN DO NOT ISSUE REFUNDS.*

Beyond the chain, the landscape looked identical to the landscape before it — the same scrubland, the same gray-brown earth, the same sky shifting from bruise to pale blue as the morning aged — but Corin understood that he was looking at a different country. The border was not geographic. It was jurisdictional.

"This is as far as I go," Eren said. "Past the chain, anyone who asks for identification is either selling something or threatening something. Don't give them identification either way. Don't mention the Archive. Don't mention Chronometry unless you're prepared to pay for the privilege — Unwoven territory runs on transaction, and information is the most expensive currency they have."

Corin opened the passenger door. The air outside was different from the Citadel's filtered atmosphere — thicker, carrying organic scents he could not all identify. Dirt and woodsmoke and meat cooking over an open fire somewhere not far. The sky was clearer here, the bruise-color fading toward an actual blue he had only seen in pre-collapse photographs.

"How do I find Orin Sect?"

"You don't find Orin Sect. Orin Sect finds you — or his people do. The Erasure Exchange operates out of a town called Harrow, about a day's walk west on the old trade road." Eren pointed at a rutted dirt path that diverged from the pavement. "Stay precisely on the road. The land on either side is mined — pre-collapse ordnance, still active. Nobody has cleared it because nobody cares about territory they can't sell."

"Mined."

"Welcome to the Unwoven territories. Where everything that doesn't generate profit gets left to rot, and everything that does gets exploited until it collapses. One more thing." Eren's tone shifted — the casual fatalism replaced by something sharper. "Your Resonance signature. The Archive can track it from a distance if you use Chronometry. Don't. No tuning, no Echo production, no frequency calibration. If you absolutely need to access the Resonance, find a shielded facility — the Unwoven run black-market tuning dens in most towns. They'll charge you. Pay whatever they ask. The alternative is the Archive triangulating your position within the hour."

Corin nodded. He stepped out. The vehicle's door closed with a sound that was more final than it should have been — the thud of metal on metal, the sealing of a compartment that would not reopen for him.

"Eren," he said. "When you go back — tell Lian the records were accurate. Her records. The ones Sable kept."

Eren regarded him for a moment. "That will mean something to her?"

"It will mean everything."

Eren nodded once and reversed the vehicle in a tight arc. The engine's growl faded westward — back toward the camp, back toward the governed districts, back toward a world Corin had left and could not return to. He watched until the vehicle was a speck, then a memory of a speck, then nothing.

He walked.

---

The old trade road was less a road than a scar — a depression in the earth where centuries of feet and wheels and pre-collapse vehicles had compressed the soil into something that no longer supported growth. Scrub brush lined both sides, gray-green and spiny, the kind of vegetation that survived in soil too poor for anything else. Beyond the brush, the land sloped into cratered terrain marked with faded warning signs: jagged red triangles on rusted metal posts, their text weathered to indecipherability. Corin stayed in the precise center of the road.

The walk gave him time to think. This was not entirely a kindness.

He thought about the five apprentices. Lian had survived by dying — faking her death, building a network, waiting in courtyards for someone who might never come. The other four had not been so lucky. Falls from observation decks that required Senior clearance. Respiratory failures consistent with prolonged Resonance exposure. Cardiac arrests with sealed autopsies. The Archive did not execute people. It simply applied procedures whose side effects were known and whose outcomes were predictable and whose documentation was classified.

He thought about Sable. A Reckoner who had learned to care about the data she protected and been deprecated for it — marked for deletion because accuracy had become inconvenient. Fifteen years alone in a sub-basement server room, surviving on degraded hardware, watching the Resonance for signs that something was wrong, witnessing deaths no one else would see. *That is all I can offer.* She had offered more than any institution ever had.

He thought about his father. A researcher who had discovered that the Archive's founding mission — protect temporal stability — had been inverted into its opposite. Every erasure weakened the Resonance. Every political purge brought the Unraveling closer. His father had developed a solution: a stabilization array that added frequencies instead of removing them. Protect timelines instead of destroying them. And for that, he had been sentenced to die across every possible version of reality.

And his mother. Her coat on the hook. Her tea warm on the table. The records of her disappearance sealed at a level that even Lian's seventeen-year intelligence network had never breached. Had she been erased, like his father? Or had she simply been deleted — once, cleanly, no Echo to mark her passing, no anomalous signature to alert a deprecated Reckoner that something had been taken?

The road curved west. The sun climbed toward noon and began its descent. Corin walked through the changing light with his father's photograph in his inner pocket and his father's research heavy against his chest. By the time the horizon began to glow with something that was not sunset, his legs were numb and his mind had cycled through every variation of grief and rage and settled, temporarily, on a single resolve: find Sect. Get the anchor frequency. Access whatever his father had left inside the Resonance. And then decide what to do with the knowledge that the institution he had trained to serve was a machine designed to destroy exactly what it claimed to protect.

---

Harrow appeared as a glow first, then a smell, then a sound.

The glow was electric light — not the muted amber of Citadel corridors but the harsh white-blue of pre-collapse fixtures powered by generators that ran on salvaged fuel and sounded like someone had weaponized asthma. The smell was a compound of things Corin's Archive-trained nose had never encountered in combination: frying oil, animal waste, chemical solvents, the cloying sweetness of alcohol distilled in someone's basement. The sound was people — hundreds of them, thousands — talking and arguing and laughing and shouting in the overlapping rhythm of a place where no single authority set the volume.

Harrow was not a town. It was an organism. Buildings stacked on buildings, materials scavenged from pre-collapse ruins and Archive supply chains and whoever had been careless enough to leave something unattended. Streets that were more like suggestions — paths worn by enough feet to acquire names, maps that existed only in the memories of residents who had no incentive to share them with strangers. Neon tubing — actual neon, salvaged from pre-collapse signage — marked establishments in pink and green and the particular blue that made your eyes ache if you looked at it too long. Bars. Brothels. Tuning dens. Erasure brokerages.

The Erasure Exchange occupied a building that had once been a pre-collapse bank. The architecture was unmistakable: marble facade, vaulted ceilings, the spatial arrogance of an institution that had considered itself permanent. The marble had been tagged with Unwoven graffiti — not art but commerce, transaction ledgers and kill counts and price lists for different categories of erasure. The cheapest: *SINGLE MOMENT — NO COLLATERAL.* The most expensive: *COMPLETE TIMELINE — POST-ERASURE CLEANUP INCLUDED.* The prices had six figures.

A man intercepted Corin at the entrance. Tall, thin, wrapped in a coat of pre-collapse smart-fabric that adjusted its insulation based on ambient temperature but had been repaired so many times it was more patch than original material.

"You're new." Not a question. "New arrivals pay a processing fee. Two Archive credits or equivalent in trade."

"I don't have credits."

"Then you don't have business in Harrow. No credit means no access to the Exchange. No access means you're another refugee with a sad story and empty pockets. We have plenty of those. They sleep in the drainage tunnels."

"I'm looking for Orin Sect."

Something behind the man's expression shifted — not surprise, but recalibration. "Everyone is looking for Orin Sect. Most of them are debt collectors. Are you a debt collector?"

"No."

"Then what are you?"

Corin considered the question. An apprentice Chronometrist who had failed his exam, fled the Citadel, crossed into lawless territory carrying a dead woman's intelligence and a dead man's research and a Reckoner's warning. A son who had watched his father die six times and was walking toward the man who had killed him. "I'm a customer."

The man studied him. Then he turned and began walking toward the vault door. "Follow. Don't touch anything. Don't talk to anyone. The Exchange charges a consultation fee. If you can't pay, they take payment in labor. If you refuse labor, they take payment in organs. This is the one moment of this conversation where I am not exaggerating."

---

The receptionist had cybernetic implants tracing her jawline in lines of blue light. She looked up when Corin entered and did not smile.

"Name and purpose."

"Corin Draven. I need information about a standing erasure operation. The target is my father."

Her implants flickered — processing, querying. "One moment." She spoke into a communicator Corin could not see.

The vault door opened.

Orin Sect's office was not what Corin had expected. He had imagined something clinical — the Chronometry equivalent of a surgical theater. Instead, the room was cluttered with objects that suggested a mind that had stopped caring about appearances approximately a decade ago. Pre-collapse artifacts in dusty display cases. Stacks of paper records that predated digital storage. A wall of monitors showing live Resonance feeds — erasure operations in progress, frequency graphs spiking and flatlining, the vital signs of timelines being removed. A coffee machine on a filing cabinet, its carafe half-full and growing a skin.

Orin Sect himself matched the room. Fifties, heavy in the way of someone who sat at a desk for long hours and ate whatever was available between meetings. Hair graying at the temples. Eyes carrying the particular exhaustion of a person who had been dealing in death long enough that it had become administrative. He did not look up from the monitor he was studying.

"Corin Draven. Your father's Echo signature has been anomalous for fourteen months — I've been paid a substantial amount of money to resolve that anomaly. So far I've failed. This is embarrassing for me professionally and very inconvenient for your father. What do you want?"

"To understand why the erasure keeps failing. And to stop it."

Sect looked up. His expression was not guarded so much as appraising — the look of someone calculating the market value of an unexpected variable. "The erasure fails because your father's Resonance signature is — I am quoting my best technicians — 'doing something that should not be physically possible.' He exists across too many frequencies simultaneously. When we erase him from one, he persists in another. When we try to erase all frequencies at once, the Resonance actively resists. It's like trying to delete a file that has been written into the operating system. The system refuses."

"Why?"

"My technicians have theories. The leading one is that your father embedded something — code, a frequency pattern, a temporal anchor — into the Resonance itself before we started the operation. The anchor prevents complete erasure. It also prevents complete life. He dies each time we attempt a new approach. He cannot stay dead. He is trapped in a loop."

Corin felt the data crystal's weight against his chest. His father's research. The stabilization array. The work Arcwright had tried to bury by erasing the researcher. "The anchor. If my father embedded something — could someone else access it?"

Sect leaned back. The exhaustion in his eyes was joined by something sharper. "Theoretically, yes. The anchor has a specific frequency. If you could tune to that frequency — which requires equipment I don't have and a Resonance sensitivity I've never seen documented — you could interact with whatever your father left there. You could potentially modify it." He paused. "Is that what you're planning?"

"I don't know yet." Corin met Sect's eyes. He was seventeen years old. Three days ago he had been an apprentice. He had no money, no allies beyond a dead woman and a deprecated Reckoner, no plan beyond the next step. But he had a data crystal containing the only known blueprint for stabilizing the Resonance, and he had an Echo that was not broken but broadcast — a working early-warning system.

"I want the erasure operation suspended. I want you to stop killing my father."

"I'm a contractor. Ren Arcwright pays for the operation. If I suspend it, I breach the contract. Arcwright hires a different broker. The operation continues. Your father keeps dying. I lose my fee." Sect's tone was flat. "Suspending the operation gains you nothing."

"Then give me the frequency. The anchor frequency."

Sect raised an eyebrow. "You understand that accessing that frequency requires tuning precision most trained Archivists can't achieve. You understand the attempt will broadcast your location to every Resonance monitoring station in both Archive and Unwoven territory. You understand that if you succeed, you'll be inside the same structure my technicians have been trying to erase for fourteen months — a structure that actively resists external manipulation. You understand I'm describing a suicide attempt with extra steps."

Corin had not understood all of that. But the fear that should have accompanied the understanding was being crowded out by something larger. His father had embedded a piece of himself into the Resonance to survive erasure. Sable had hidden in a sub-basement for fifteen years to protect data no one was supposed to see. Lian had faked her death and built an intelligence network to wait for a sixth apprentice whose Echo would be strong enough.

They had all made choices. Corin could make one too.

"I understand," he said. "Give me the frequency."

Sect studied him. Then he reached into a drawer — not the one with a weapon, but the one with snacks — and removed a protein bar, which he unwrapped and ate half of in a single bite.

"The frequency is 47.2-omega," he said, chewing. "Edge of the Resonance's stable band. Most equipment can't reach it. There's a tuning den three blocks west — basement of the building with the green neon. The operator has mechanical hands and a reconstructed face. She's done work for me before. Tell her Sect sent you." He slid a data chip across the desk. "This is the full erasure operation log. My technicians' notes. Failure analyses. It might help."

Corin picked up the chip. "Why are you helping me?"

Sect finished the protein bar. "Because I've been failing to kill your father for fourteen months and I don't like failing. It implies my prices don't match my capabilities. If you succeed — if you stabilize the anchor, break the loop, do whatever impossible thing you intend to do — I get something more valuable than Arcwright's fee. I get the answer to a question I've been asking for fourteen months. What exactly is your father doing inside the Resonance? Curiosity is the only currency in this territory more expensive than information." He smiled. The smile was not warm. "Good luck. If you survive, send word. I'd frame it."

---

The tuning den was a converted basement beneath a building marked with green neon in the shape of a frequency wave. The operator was a woman with mechanical hands — articulated metal fingers moving with the precision of long practice — and a face that had been reconstructed at least twice. Burn scars traced her jawline where the synth-skin grafts had not fully taken.

She looked at the frequency Corin wrote down. Then she looked at him. "47.2-omega is outside the stable band. You'll burn out the array. Possibly yourself with it."

"I know the risks."

"Everyone who comes to Harrow knows the risks. Most of them die anyway." She shrugged and began calibrating the tuning bed — a reclined platform surrounded by copper coils that had been salvaged from at least six different pre-collapse devices and wired together with more optimism than precision. "Lie down. Try not to scream. It disturbs the other customers."

Corin lay down on the bed. The ceiling above was water-stained and cracked, a map of damage that had been accumulating since before he was born. The generator in the walls coughed and stabilized. The operator's mechanical hands moved across the control panel, dialing frequencies with the precision of someone who had done this thousands of times for people who had equally desperate reasons.

"Frequency 47.2-omega," she said. "Engaging in five seconds."

Corin closed his eyes.

The Resonance found him.

The frequency was jagged — wrong-feeling, discordant, a note played on an instrument dropped too many times. Corin felt his mind reaching into the harmonic field, felt the Echo trying to form, felt the same fracture pattern that had shattered his exam Echo threatening to repeat.

And then he was through.

---

The Echo resolved around him. Not the six simultaneous deaths. Not the fractured fragments. Something else — something his father had built inside the Resonance itself fourteen months ago as a failsafe against the erasure he knew was coming.

A room. Pre-collapse architecture, intact — the clean lines and warm materials of an era that had not yet learned to fear what it could create. A desk with a terminal that still had power, its screen displaying waveforms Corin recognized from his father's research notes. Bookshelves lined with physical volumes — real books, their spines cracked from use, the kind of objects the Archive preserved in climate-controlled vaults and never allowed anyone to touch. A window that looked out onto a Resonance geography that was not any single timeline but a composite of all of them: cities overlapping with forests, oceans bleeding into deserts, the harmonic blend of what-was and what-could-be rendered as terrain.

And at the desk, a man with a face Corin recognized from a photograph and a mirror.

His father looked up.

"Corin," he said. His voice was calm — the calm of someone who had been waiting and was prepared for the person he was waiting for. "You're early. I calculated another six months before your Echo would be strong enough to find this frequency."

Corin could not speak. The room was real — more real than any Echo he had ever produced, more stable than any Resonance projection in the textbooks, more solid than the world he had left behind in the tuning den. His father had not just embedded a fragment. He had built a sanctuary.

"Son." His father stood and walked around the desk. The resemblance was stronger in motion — the same angular jaw, the same way of holding his shoulders, the same intensity behind the eyes. "The Resonance is dying. The Archive is lying. And the only person who can stabilize either of them is currently standing in my office with his mouth open."

Corin found his voice. It was smaller than he wanted. "You're alive."

"Complicated. I'm alive in the sense that I'm conscious and speaking to you. I'm dead in every timeline where Orin Sect has completed an erasure attempt — fourteen timelines as of this morning. The fifteenth is in progress. I have maybe an hour before this instance also dies." He placed a hand on Corin's shoulder. The pressure was faint, already fading at the edges. "But the anchor will persist. I built it to persist. And you're here, which means you can access it. Which means you can do what I couldn't."

"What couldn't you do?"

"Stabilize the Resonance before Arcwright erased me. I was close — working theory, partial implementation, a prototype that functioned in simulation. But Arcwright discovered what I was building before I could test it at scale. He couldn't just kill me — too public, too many questions from the research division. So he paid Sect to erase me from every possible timeline. What he didn't know was that I'd anticipated him. The anchor protects a fragment of my consciousness and all of my research. The research is yours now."

Corin looked at the terminal. The screen displayed waveforms — Resonance frequencies, harmonic maps, stability projections — in a mathematical language he recognized but did not fully understand. Seventeen years of work compressed into data hidden inside the thing the Archive was destroying.

"I don't know if I can do this," he said.

"Neither did I." His father's hand on his shoulder was barely perceptible now — the pressure of light rather than flesh. "I tried anyway. You have the crystal. You have Lian's network — I sent her the data two days before Sect started the operation. You have Sable. I helped her hide in the sub-basement when the deprecation order came. You have Iris and the Timelost and every person the Archive has tried to erase who refused to stay erased. You are not alone. You were never alone."

The room flickered. A fracture appeared in the window — the same glass-crack pattern that had shattered Corin's Exam Echo. The fifteenth erasure was accelerating.

"Time," his father said. "The Resonance is a harmonic system. Erasures remove frequencies. Removing frequencies causes instability. Instability causes cascade failure. Cascade failure causes Unraveling. You cannot fix it by erasing more. You fix it by adding frequencies — by protecting timelines instead of destroying them. The stabilization array I designed does exactly that. The blueprint is on the crystal. The frequencies are mapped. You have everything you—"

The fracture spread. His father's voice distorted, the harmonics pulling apart.

"—need. I love you. Tell your mother I'm sorry I couldn't find her before—"

The room shattered.

Corin fell — through the fractured window, through the composite landscape, through the dying frequencies of an anchor that would persist even as the man who built it faded. The Resonance rushed past him like wind through a structure that was losing integrity, and in the rush he heard something he had not noticed before: a second frequency beneath the chaos. Lower. Steadier. A harmonic baseline that the erasure could not touch.

His father's anchor. Holding.

---

He opened his eyes on the tuning bed. The operator's mechanical hands were gripping his shoulders. Her reconstructed face was inches from his own, and her expression — concern, actual concern — was the most human thing Corin had seen since he left the Timelost camp.

"Your vitals crashed," she said. "Seven seconds. Clinically dead. Then you weren't." She released his shoulders. Her mechanical fingers left faint impressions in the fabric of his coat. "What did you see?"

Corin sat up. The room spun and stabilized. His father's photograph was still in his inner pocket. The data crystal was still heavy against his chest. Sect's data chip was in the opposite pocket, and the frequency — 47.2-omega — was still burning in his mind like an afterimage of light.

"Everything," he said.

The operator stepped back. Her hands folded into a gesture that might have been respect or a ward against misfortune — in Harrow, Corin was learning, the distinction was negligible.

"Where do you go now?" she asked.

Corin stood. His legs held. Outside, the Harrow night was still blazing with neon and transaction and the endless hum of a territory where everything was for sale. Somewhere to the east, the Citadel of Echoes rose black against a sky he could not see from here. Somewhere beneath it, Sable was watching the Resonance, witnessing deaths that no one else would witness. Somewhere at the edge of the governed districts, Lian was waiting in a courtyard, feeding pages to a fire, watching the entrance for someone who might never return. Somewhere in a shipping-container hub, Iris was writing names in a ledger with a pen that leaked — building a memorial one entry at a time for people whose timelines had been erased and who refused to be forgotten.

And somewhere in the Resonance, a man who had been killed fifteen times waited for his son to do the impossible.

Corin walked out of the tuning den into the Harrow night. The neon was bright. The transactions were endless. The air smelled of frying oil and chemical solvents and the particular sweetness of alcohol distilled in basements, and for the first time since he pressed his palm to the tuning plate in Marek's exam chamber, he knew exactly what he was going to do.

The Citadel had trained him to access the Resonance. His father had taught him how to protect it. And Ren Arcwright — Senior Archivist, Council of Seven, the man who had paid to erase a researcher who threatened his power — was about to learn what happened when you tried to delete something that had been written into the operating system of reality itself.

Corin Draven was going home.

chapters.title: Chapter 7: The Weight of Going Back
chapters.text:
The Timelost camp smelled different at night.

Corin had noticed it on the walk back from Harrow — not the absence of something but the presence, the accumulated scent of thousands of people cooking over salvaged fires, washing in rainwater, living close together in a community built from the things other people had thrown away. It was not unpleasant. It was human in a way the Citadel had never been. The Citadel smelled of ozone and cold stone and the faint copper tang of tuning plates that had been used so many times their surfaces were worn smooth. The Citadel smelled like sterility pretending to be order. The camp smelled like people who had survived erasure and were still here.

Eren had dropped him at the eastern edge of the camp an hour before dawn and driven away without saying goodbye. Corin understood. The man existed with no past — goodbyes were for people who expected to see each other again, and Eren had stopped expecting that a long time ago.

Lian was waiting in the administration hub.

She sat cross-legged on a salvaged cargo crate, cloth-wrapped hands resting on her knees, her gray braid pulled over one shoulder. The pre-collapse reader Iris had been modifying was open on the table beside her, its screen casting blue light across the shipping-container walls. She looked up when Corin entered and for a moment said nothing. The silence was not awkward. It was the silence of someone who had waited seventeen years for a sixth apprentice and was now taking inventory of what she had received.

"You found him," she said. It was not a question.

"In the Resonance. He built an anchor — a constructed space inside the Layer. He's conscious. He's been there the whole time." Corin pulled the data crystal from his pocket. It caught the reader's blue light and scattered it across the walls in geometric patterns. "He gave me this. Seventeen years of research. The stabilization array blueprint. Everything he couldn't publish before the erasure order."

Lian's expression did not change, but her hands tightened on her knees. The cloth strips creaked. "And the erasure?"

"The fifteenth attempt completed while I was there. The sixteenth has already started. Sect says Arcwright is funding it personally now — six times the original budget. He wants my father gone before anyone else finds out what the research proves."

"What does it prove?"

"That erasures are killing the Resonance. Every timeline the Archive removes weakens the harmonic structure. At the current rate, we have about three years before cascade failure — all possible timelines collapse into a single unstable present. Arcwright knows. He's been covering it up."

Lian was silent for a long moment. Outside, someone started a generator. The sound was rhythmic and mechanical and entirely human — someone had built this thing from salvage and kept it running past its intended lifespan because the alternative was silence and darkness and the slow forgetting that came with both.

"Seventeen years," she said. "I knew Aldric was right. He was always right about the Resonance — the way he talked about frequencies, harmonic baselines, the things no one else could hear. He said the Layer was a living system. He said erasures were a wound, not a solution. And I believed him. But I never..." She stopped. Her eyes were wet. Lian Asher did not cry easily — she had survived seven calibrations and faked her own death and built an intelligence network from a courtyard lean-to — but she was crying now, and she did not bother to hide it. "I never thought his own erasure would be the evidence I needed to believe him completely."

Corin sat down on the crate beside her. The data crystal was still in his hand. It was heavier than it looked — pre-collapse manufacture, crystal lattice that scattered light in patterns that shifted when you turned it, like something alive was trapped inside. His father had carried this. His father had built the anchor. His father had known, for two weeks before the erasure order, that someone was coming to delete him — and had spent those two weeks preparing, not to save himself but to preserve the research that could save everyone else.

"What do you need?" Lian asked. Her voice was steady again. The tears were still on her face but the question was operational. She had shifted from grieving to planning in the space of a breath, and Corin understood that this was how she had survived. Grief in intervals. Plans in the spaces between.

"To get back inside the Citadel. I need access to Sable's network — she has calibration records that prove the neurological damage, and she can help me map the approach to the west wing. And I need forged credentials. The kind that pass automated scanning."

"The camp doesn't have forge-grade equipment. The Timelost trade in information, not documents." Lian hesitated. "But I know someone who does."

"The hesitation suggests I won't like her."

"You won't. She's an Unwoven broker. Transactional, cold, morally flexible in ways that would make Orin Sect look principled. But she owes me a debt — a significant one — and she's good at what she does." Lian stood, brushing dust from her salvaged coat. "Her name is Wynn Calder. I'll arrange a meeting. In the meantime, there's someone else you should talk to."

"Who?"

"The cook."

Corin blinked. "I'm not hungry."

"I didn't say you should eat. I said you should talk to him." Lian was already walking toward the hub's entrance. "His name is Theron. He's been baking bread in this camp for eight years. Before that, he was Senior Archivist Deyan Halcott. He signed calibration orders for one of the five apprentices. Then he faked his death in a tunnel collapse, erased his own personnel file, and has been feeding intelligence to the Timelost resistance under false identities ever since. If anyone knows how to get you back inside the Citadel, it's him."

She left Corin standing in the hub with the data crystal heavy in one hand and the photograph of his father warm in the other, the reader's blue light still scattering geometric patterns across the walls like the Resonance itself was trying to draw a map.

---

The communal kitchen was a long tent near the camp's southern edge, its canvas patched with Archive waste fabric and pre-collapse polymer sheeting. Even from outside, the smell reached him — not just yeast but the char from the iron range's fire, the dry-grass smell of old flour absorbed into the tent walls over years of daily baking, the fainter underlayer of woodsmoke that had been part of the canvas so long it had become structural. Heat radiated through the walls in slow pulses, each one timed to the range's burn cycle. The camp said the sourdough starter dated to before the Unraveling — that someone, in the chaos of the collapse, had carried a jar of living culture out of a burning city and handed it forward, camp to camp, survival to survival, until it arrived here. Corin did not know if this was true. He knew that the bread it produced was good, and that Theron tended it with an attention that suggested the story mattered even if the facts were uncertain. Inside, the air was thick with flour dust and the smell of yeast that had been kept alive across generations. A man stood at a salvaged metal table, kneading dough with practiced, unhurried movements. He was broad and gray-haired, with a face that looked like it had been carved for a more decisive man and then softened by years of something that was not quite regret and not quite peace.

He did not look up when Corin entered.

"You're Corin," he said. His voice was low, rough at the edges, the voice of someone who had spent decades not raising it. "You need to get back inside the Citadel. I know three ways. Two of them will get you caught. The third is theoretically possible."

Corin stopped a few feet from the table. "Lian told you I was coming."

"Lian didn't need to tell me. I've been watching you since you arrived." Theron folded the dough, pressed, folded again. His hands moved with the muscle memory of someone who had been doing this for years. "The eastern sector of the Citadel has been decommissioned for eleven years. The Archive sealed it after a calibration accident — three technicians dead, massive Resonance bleed, the whole wing rendered unstable. But the maintenance shafts in that sector were never properly secured. There's an access point in the sub-basement — a ventilation duct that was cut into the eastern wall during the original construction and never added to the official blueprints. It's narrow. You'll have to crawl. But it bypasses the automated scanning checkpoints and comes out three corridors from the decommissioned calibration annex."

Corin studied the man. The details Lian had provided were assembling themselves into a picture that did not fit the man in front of him. Senior Archivist meant decades of service. Senior Archivist meant access to the Council chambers, the sealed records, the Reckoner network. Senior Archivist meant power. And this man — flour on his apron, dough under his fingernails, voice calibrated to the exact register of someone who did not want to be noticed — had walked away from all of it.

"How do you know about the ventilation duct?"

Theron's hands paused over the dough. The pause was brief — less than a second — but it was the first break in his rhythm since Corin had entered. "Because I approved the work order that created it. Thirty-one years ago. I was the facilities supervisor for the eastern sector before I was promoted to calibration oversight." He resumed kneading. "The duct was installed to vent chemical byproducts from a pre-collapse analyzer the Archive recovered from the ruins. The analyzer was decommissioned two years later. The duct was never documented. The facilities team that installed it was purged during a Council restructuring. The only people who know about it are me and the structural engineer who signed the installation report — and she died of calibration-related neurological failure seventeen years ago."

Corin felt the weight of the data crystal in his pocket. "Lian says you signed calibration orders for one of the apprentices. The second one."

Theron's hands stopped again. This time they did not resume.

"Yes."

"Did you know what calibration did?"

A longer pause. Outside the tent, someone laughed — a child, high and quick. The sound was jarring against the stillness inside.

"Not at first." Theron's voice was quieter now, the roughness giving way to something flatter. "The official line was that calibration was therapeutic — a corrective procedure to stabilize anomalous Resonance signatures before they caused psychological damage. The apprentices were told it was for their own protection. I believed that. I wanted to believe that. I had spent twenty-three years in the Archive by then. I had built a career on the assumption that we were the good ones — the preservers, the stabilizers. When an apprentice's Echo deviated from standard, calibration was the recommended response. Every textbook said so. Every senior Chronometrist confirmed it. The Council had approved the protocol at the highest level."

He turned to face Corin for the first time. His eyes were hazel and tired and entirely still — the eyes of someone who had learned to watch without appearing to watch, a habit from years of surveillance and decades of hiding.

"By the third apprentice, I knew. The neurological damage was cumulative. The memory suppression was not therapeutic — it was erasure. We weren't treating them. We were deleting the parts we couldn't control." He looked down at his hands. They were covered in flour. "I signed the order for the second apprentice. His name was Deyan Koss. He was seventeen. I signed the paper that authorized his calibration, and I told myself it was procedure, and three years later I read the report that classified his death as 'complications following a routine procedure,' and I knew it was a lie. And I did nothing."

"What changed?"

"Nothing." Theron's voice cracked on the word. "That's the point. Nothing changed. I kept signing. I kept filing. I kept attending Council briefings where Arcwright presented calibration statistics as evidence of 'improved Resonance stability outcomes.' I kept looking at the sealed autopsy reports and telling myself I was misinterpreting the data. And then the fifth apprentice died — a sixteen-year-old girl named Isara Fell whose Resonance sensitivity was so extreme they couldn't calibrate her below the detection threshold, so they deliberately set the calibration to a lethal frequency and called it 'complications.'" He wiped his hands on his apron. The gesture was automatic, a cook's reflex. "I did not blow the whistle. I did not resign in protest. I faked my death in a tunnel collapse that happened to coincide with a maintenance inspection I was supposed to be supervising, and I walked away. I have been walking away for twenty-two years."

Corin considered the man in front of him — the flour, the gray hair, the stillness of his eyes, the tremor in his left hand that he was trying to conceal by gripping the edge of the table. He was not a hero. He was not a villain. He was something more uncomfortable: a person who had done the wrong thing for the right reasons and the right thing for the wrong reasons and was now standing in a camp kitchen at dawn, offering information to a stranger because it was the only form of atonement he could manage.

"You said the third way was theoretically possible. What makes it theoretical?"

Theron released the table. The tremor was still there, faint but visible. "The duct is in the sealed eastern sector. The eastern sector was sealed because of a Resonance bleed — the calibration accident I mentioned. The bleed is still active. The ambient Resonance in that sector is unstable — frequencies fluctuating at unpredictable intervals. If you access the Resonance while you're in there — if your signature registers on the Archive's monitoring grid — the instability will amplify your signal. Every Chronometrist in the Citadel will know exactly where you are within sixty seconds."

"So I can't access the Resonance inside."

"Correct. You can navigate the duct. You can reach the decommissioned annex. You can even reach a hidden terminal that connects to the old Reckoner network — if your friend in the sub-basement has kept the routing active. But you cannot touch the Resonance while you're inside. No tuning. No Echo access. No signature verification. You will be blind in the one sense the Archive trained you to rely on."

Corin was silent for a moment. The eastern sector. A Resonance bleed. No Chronometry. A terminal that might still connect to Sable. It was not a plan. It was a series of conditions that could, if navigated precisely, become a plan.

"The credentials," he said. "The automated scanning — forged documents need to pass Resonance signature verification. Can your broker do that?"

"Wynn Calder can do anything that has a price. The question is whether the price is one you're willing to pay." Theron turned back to his dough. "She'll meet you at the border waystation tomorrow. Lian is arranging it. Bring something to trade — information, not money. Wynn's currency is what you know, not what you have."

Corin started to leave, then stopped. "The fifth apprentice. Isara Fell. What did she see?"

Theron's hands paused again. This time the pause was longer. "Something in the deep Resonance layers. She called it 'a heartbeat that isn't human.' The Archive classified the report and recalibrated her at double the standard intensity. She was sixteen."

"Did you believe her?"

Theron did not answer. The dough stretched under his hands, folded, stretched again. Outside, the sun was rising over the salvage-built shelters, casting long shadows through the patched canvas. Corin left the kitchen and walked back toward the administration hub, and the question hung in the flour-dusted air behind him, unanswered, like an Echo that had broken free of its timeline and was waiting for someone to notice it was still here.

chapters.title: Chapter 8: The Broker's Price
chapters.text:
The border waystation was not a building. It was a condition.

The original structure had been a pre-collapse transit depot — a long concrete shell with a curved roof that had once sheltered travelers waiting for trains that no longer ran. The tracks were gone now, salvaged for metal decades ago, and the depot had been converted into something that was not quite a market and not quite a refuge. Merchants set up temporary stalls in the mornings and dismantled them by dusk. Couriers exchanged messages that could not be transmitted through the Archive's monitored channels. People with reasons to avoid both Archive patrols and Unwoven tax collectors used the waystation as a neutral zone — a place where no one asked questions because everyone had questions they did not want asked.

The smell was the first thing that registered: machine oil, old stone, something sweeter underneath that Corin identified after a moment as pre-collapse adhesive — the specific synthetic odor of a structure that had been patched and re-patched across two centuries until the original material was buried under layers of repair. The sound was a low, constant murmur, a dozen simultaneous conversations in a room designed for transit rather than accommodation, all of them pitched below a threshold that would carry to the wrong ears. No one shouted. No one gestured broadly. Even the child selling cups of something hot from a ceramic urn — the urn's style suggested it might have been older than the Unraveling, its glaze worn to a matte finish by generations of handling — called out prices in the careful, measured voice of someone who had grown up knowing that noise was a form of address, and addresses could be answered by the wrong people.

Corin arrived at mid-morning. Lian had given him directions wrapped in a warning: *Don't try to negotiate. Don't try to charm. Don't try to make her like you. She will take the meeting because of the debt. She will help you because she is contractually obligated to. She will not be your friend. She will not be your ally. She will be a resource you access exactly once before the obligation is discharged and the door closes. Use her accordingly.*

He had repeated the warning to himself three times on the walk over. He was repeating it again when he stepped through the waystation's main entrance.

The main hall was lit by narrow windows set high in the concrete walls, their glass long gone, their frames casting a grid of pale morning light across the cracked floor. A few merchants were setting up — a woman arranging salvaged pre-collapse electronics on a blanket, moving each piece with the practiced care of someone who had inherited the collection rather than purchased it; a man with a cart of Archive-grade medical supplies (expired, but still effective if you knew how to compensate for date-degraded potency, which this man evidently did, based on the small queue forming at his cart); the child with the ceramic urn, who looked at Corin with the direct, unsentimental appraisal of someone who had spent enough time in neutral zones to distinguish between people who were safe, people who were dangerous, and people who were not yet certain which they were. Corin, apparently, fell into the third category. The child went back to calling out prices.

Wynn Calder was standing near the far wall, examining a display of pre-collapse wristwatches with an expression that suggested she had already calculated the resale value of every item and dismissed them all as beneath her.

She was exactly as Lian had described: sharp-faced, dark-haired, dressed in a black smart-fabric coat that moved differently from normal cloth — the fibers adjusting themselves in response to temperature and motion, a piece of pre-collapse technology that would have cost more than most Unwoven residents earned in a year. Her hair was cut in a severe asymmetrical bob, shaved close on the right side, longer on the left. Three small dots were tattooed behind her right ear. A thin silver chain was visible at her collar, disappearing beneath the high neck of her shirt.

She looked at Corin the way someone appraises a used piece of equipment — assessing wear, estimating value, calculating whether the purchase was worth the maintenance.

"You're smaller than your bounty suggests," she said. Her voice was clipped, efficient, the voice of someone who had learned to communicate in transactions rather than conversations. "Arcwright's paying sixty thousand credits for your calibration. I could retire."

"Lian says you owe her."

Something flickered across Wynn's face. The expression was gone before Corin could identify it — not guilt, not gratitude, something more complicated that she had spent years learning not to show.

"I remember what I owe." She reached into her coat and produced a small scanner — pre-collapse manufacture, sleek and functional, its screen casting pale blue light across her fingers. "Left palm. Press and hold for three seconds."

Corin hesitated. "What are you using it for?"

"Your Resonance signature has a unique frequency pattern. Every Chronometrist's does. The Archive's scanning checkpoints match signatures against a database of authorized personnel. My credentials need to mimic your signature closely enough to pass automated verification while encoding false identity data in the metadata layer. To do that, I need a baseline reading." She held the scanner steady. "I'm not selling your signature, apprentice. The people who would pay for it are the same people who would pay me to deliver you — and Lian's debt is worth more to me than their money."

Corin pressed his palm against the scanner. The surface was cold — not the comfortable cold of the Citadel's climate-controlled interiors, but a sharper cold, the cold of something engineered to operate in conditions that human bodies were not designed for. Pre-collapse technology had a specific quality to it that Archive-built equipment did not: a precision that felt indifferent rather than functional, as if the device was measuring him not as a person but as a data set, and had no particular opinion about what the data meant. Three seconds. Four. The scanner beeped once, a tone pitched slightly lower than Archive-standard equipment.

He felt it briefly — a pulse against his palm, the device reading the frequencies his body generated and suppressed simultaneously, the echo-pattern of a Chronometric sensitivity that had never been properly calibrated because every time the Archive had tried to calibrate it, something in the process had broken wrong. He could not tell what the scanner recorded. He could not tell whether his signature had just been copied or simply noted. The cold faded when he removed his palm.

Wynn pocketed it without looking at the reading. "Two days. The credentials will be delivered to the Timelost camp. Full documentation package: identification, access authorization, shift assignment, personnel history that will hold up to a standard records query. I can't guarantee it passes a deep audit — if someone pulls your file manually and cross-references the training records, the discrepancies will surface. But automated scanning will flag you as a mid-level maintenance technician assigned to the eastern sector decommissioning team. It's a dead-end posting — no one checks those records carefully."

"What's the cost?"

"The debt covers the credentials. The safe passage information is extra." She reached into another pocket and withdrew a data chip — smaller than the one Sect had given Corin, its surface unmarked. "This contains the current patrol schedules for all transit corridors between the camp and the Citadel's outer checkpoints. Updated three days ago. Verified against Archive dispatch logs. The patrol gaps are annotated — time windows where you can move without being stopped. It also contains the Archive's current watchlist of flagged Resonance signatures, including yours. Arcwright updated it yesterday. Your description has been distributed to every checkpoint in the district."

She held out the chip.

"And in exchange?"

Wynn's expression did not change, but something shifted in her posture — a fractional straightening of the shoulders, a conscious adjustment. "In exchange, you don't mention this meeting to anyone. Not Iris. Not your ghost driver. Not the camp cook who used to be an Archivist. When you get caught — and you will get caught, eventually, because people like you always do — you don't mention my name. You don't mention my operation. You don't mention the waystation. You take whatever the Archive does to you and you keep my involvement out of it. That's the cost."

Corin took the chip. It was small and light and completely unmarked — the kind of object that could be lost in a pocket and never found again. The kind of object that could save your life or end it, depending on whether the information it contained was accurate.

"Why are you really helping me?"

Wynn's eyes narrowed. For a moment the transactional mask slipped — not into warmth but into something closer to assessment, the look of someone recalculating an equation that had produced an unexpected variable.

"Because five years ago, an Archive patrol stopped my daughter on a road outside Harrow. She was two years old. She has a condition — temporal-phase dissonance. Her body flickers between solidity and translucence without warning. The patrol would have taken her. They would have classified her as a medical anomaly and shipped her to a research facility, and I would have spent the rest of my life trying to find out where they had taken her and what they were doing to her and whether she was still alive." Wynn's voice did not change, but she went still — the particular stillness of someone replaying a specific fear they have rehearsed so many times it has become architectural. "Her name is Myra. She is seven now. She flickers less. The doctors in Harrow think it will resolve by adolescence. They do not know for certain. They have never seen a case like hers survive to adolescence, because cases like hers are usually taken."

She exhaled once, controlled, the precise management of a breath that might have become something else.

"Lian Asher was traveling the same road. She hid Myra in a smuggling compartment built into the floor of a transport vehicle and told the patrol she was a courier carrying medical supplies. She stayed in Harrow for three days until I returned. She did not ask for payment. She did not ask for anything. Myra asked for her name. Lian told her. Myra still says it sometimes, when she wakes from the dreams that happen when the flicker is bad. *Lian. Lian.* Like a talisman."

Wynn's voice had not changed — still clipped, still efficient — but her hand had moved to her collar, her fingers resting on the silver chain where it disappeared beneath her shirt. The gesture was unconscious. It was the only uncalculated thing she had done since Corin arrived.

"That debt is not discharged by forged credentials," she said. "But it's a start. Take the chip. The patrol gaps are accurate. If you follow them, you'll reach the Citadel without being stopped. What happens after that is your problem."

She turned and walked toward the waystation's exit, her smart-fabric coat adjusting itself to the morning chill, her boots clicking on the cracked concrete floor. At the doorway she stopped.

"Corin."

He turned.

"Arcwright doubled the bounty this morning. Seventy thousand credits, plus a commission for the operative who brings you in alive. He's hiring external contractors — Unwoven trackers who specialize in Resonance signature hunting. They'll be operating by tomorrow. If you're going to get inside the Citadel, do it tonight."

She did not wait for a response. The doorway swallowed her, and the morning light fell across the empty space where she had been standing, and Corin was left holding a data chip that contained the difference between reaching the Citadel and dying on the road.

He left the waystation and started walking back toward the camp.

Lian had said: *She will not be your friend.* She had been right. What had happened in that neutral-zone hall was not friendship — it was a transaction concluded, a debt converted from metaphysical to practical, a woman calculating what she owed and paying it in exactly the currency the moment required. And yet: *Myra. Myra. Like a talisman.* He had not expected that. He had not expected the silver chain or the stillness or the way a completely self-possessed woman could become briefly, precisely vulnerable through a single unconscious movement of her hand. The Archive's ethics textbooks described human behavior as predictable within a range of parameters — motivation, context, expected utility, response optimization. They did not mention what happened when a debt was not owed to the institution but to the person who had once, on a road outside Harrow, decided that a two-year-old girl was worth three days of her life with nothing asked in return.

The morning sun was higher now, the shadows shorter, the air warming into something that would have felt almost comfortable in the Archive's temperature-controlled corridors. Out here it felt like weather — unpredictable, impersonal, the particular warmth of a day that would reach its peak and then cool regardless of what it meant to anyone standing in it.

He passed a vendor with a tray of dried fruit — pre-collapse varieties, the kind preserved in Archive controlled-environment storage that were working their way into the Unwoven markets as the storage facilities aged past useful function and were abandoned to whoever could find and empty them. The vendor called a price. Corin kept walking. The Archive had trained him to process sensory input for informational value: assess, categorize, discard. The smell of those dried fruits — something sweeter than he could name, a cultivar that no longer existed in any living garden — was not informational. It was just a smell. He registered it, walked past it, and found himself half a block later thinking about what it would mean to live in a world where that smell was ordinary, where someone had grown the fruit and preserved it and sold it not as a relic but as Tuesday. The world the Archive had erased to maintain stability. The world Arcwright said could not be allowed to exist. Wynn Calder's daughter lived in that world, partially — a girl who flickered between solidity and translucence and still asked for the name of the woman who had hidden her in a smuggling compartment five years ago, as if the name were a tether and the tether kept her from dissolving into the next phase entirely.

Ahead of him, the road ran east toward the Archive-governed districts and the obsidian spires of the Citadel and the man who had been trying to kill his father across fourteen timelines. Behind him, the waystation was already filling with the day's commerce — merchants arranging their goods, couriers exchanging packages, the child with the ceramic urn calling out prices in a voice that carried across the empty tracks.

And somewhere in the middle distance, the Unwoven tracker teams were already moving — military-grade portable arrays, Resonance signatures tagged and triangulated, approach vectors calculated with the same computational precision the Archive brought to every problem that required erasing something inconvenient. Seventy thousand credits. He was worth seventy thousand credits and a new fleet of contractors because he had stood in an exam chamber and produced an Echo that shouldn't exist, which meant the thing his father had spent his life protecting was real, which meant the Archive's entire justification for what it did to people was built on exactly the kind of lie that had killed five apprentices before him.

Corin walked faster. The data chip was in his pocket. The patrol gaps were accurate, Wynn had said — as if the accuracy of the data was what mattered, when what actually mattered was that a woman he would never see again had just told him her daughter's name, and somehow that felt more important than the bounty and the tracker teams and all seven of the impossible things he would need to survive in order to do anything useful with the next three days of his life.

chapters.title: Chapter 9: What the Cook Remembers
chapters.text:
Theron was waiting for him at the kitchen tent's entrance.

It was late afternoon, the sun dropping toward the camp's western edge, the light slanting through the patched canvas in long amber rectangles. The camp was quieter than usual — some disturbance at the eastern checkpoint had drawn the usual crowd of onlookers and rumor-spreaders, leaving the southern edge almost deserted. Theron was not kneading dough. He was sitting on a cargo crate outside the tent, a steaming cup of something dark in his hands, his gray hair pulled back in its usual knot, his eyes fixed on a point somewhere past the camp's perimeter fence.

He did not look up when Corin approached. He was watching a group of children on the far side of the camp, playing some game that involved a rope and elaborate rules and a great deal of negotiation about what the rules actually were. One of them — the girl who sang, the one with Deyan Koss's melody still in her repertoire — was explaining something to the others with the patient authority of someone who had been playing this game longer than anyone and had, somewhere in the past year, transitioned from participant to keeper of the rules. She was eight, maybe nine. She had not been born into this camp. She had arrived here from somewhere that no longer existed, carrying nothing, including the language she sometimes sang in, which was not a language anymore so much as a frequency — a set of sounds that, to a trained ear, contained information.

Corin sat on the adjacent crate without being invited. Between them, the camp extended westward.

"Wynn Calder gave you the credentials." It was not a question.

"She gave me more than that. Arcwright's hiring Resonance trackers. They'll be operational by tomorrow. I need to be inside the Citadel tonight."

Theron nodded slowly. The movement was heavy, weighted — the nod of someone who had spent decades processing bad news and had developed a physical economy around it. "Then we need to talk about what you're going to find when you get there."

He set down his cup and looked directly at Corin for the first time. His hands were steady now — steadier than they had been in the kitchen tent, as if talking about something specific was easier than talking about himself.

"I told you I signed the calibration order for Deyan Koss. The second apprentice. What I didn't tell you is what he found, or what I did when I found out."

He paused. The children across the camp were still playing, still negotiating the rules of their rope game. The girl who sang was explaining something — her hands illustrating, her face patient with the authority of someone who had held this particular knowledge longer than anyone else in the group.

"Deyan was seventeen. Mathematical prodigy — the kind of apprentice who corrected his instructors' equations in the margins of their own textbooks. His anomalous Echo showed Timelost refugees being transported to an Unwoven mining operation. The site had explicit Archive approval — a Council-issued resource extraction permit. The Archive was not just ignoring Timelost exploitation. It was issuing permits for it. Licensing it. Processing the revenue." Theron's voice was flat and careful, the voice of someone reciting facts that have been held in the body for so long they have become part of its structure. "He showed me the documentation. Budget line items. Transfer orders signed by Council members whose names you would recognize from the Hall of Dedicated Service. I told him he was misreading it. That there was a procedural context he didn't have the clearance to understand. That if he reported it formally, I would personally review the documentation and escalate through the appropriate channels."

Theron stopped.

"I was thirty-nine. I had been in the Archive for eighteen years. I had seven completed erasure operations on my record, all approved, all logged as *scheduled harmonic maintenance.* I told him the system had a process and the process would work. Three days later I countersigned his calibration order."

The silence that followed was not uncomfortable. It was just the silence.

"Deyan was too smart to trust me. Before the calibration, he encoded what he'd discovered into a children's nursery rhyme — a simple tune with melodic intervals that mapped to Resonance frequency coordinates. The rhyme spread through the Timelost camps, passed from child to child. Its true meaning was hidden in plain hearing. The song you heard in the camp when you arrived — the girl singing in a language that doesn't exist anymore — contains fragments of Deyan's encoding, still circulating sixty years later. He died at twenty-six. His seventh calibration was set to a lethal frequency. He knew it was coming. He had known since the second one. He spent nine years carrying what he knew and putting it into a form that the Archive could not suppress without suppressing music itself. The melody he chose was not arbitrary. The oldest Timelost camps practiced a twelve-station mourning ceremony called the Liturgy of the First Unraveling — performed after a timeline was lost, each station marking a phase of collective grief. The twelfth station was called *The Return*: the one who carries the death of the father and refuses to let go, passing the weight forward until someone capable of receiving it arrives. Deyan had fitted his frequency coordinates into that frame and released them into a living tradition. The Archive had looked at the melody and heard lamentation. They had not heard the map."

Theron reached into the pocket of his work apron. What he withdrew was small and battered: a chapbook, maybe forty pages, its spine split and repaired with a strip of adhesive tape that had dried and begun to peel. The cover bore a handwritten title and below it, inked in careful strokes, the circle-and-frequency-line symbol of the Chronometric Commons — the open-access movement whose members the Archive had spent two decades erasing from every public record, classifying their shared Resonance libraries as criminal infrastructure. The last Commoners willing to operate openly had been erased from every timeline twelve years before Corin's birth.

"Deyan gave me this," Theron said. "Three days before I signed his order. He told me: if you believe what's in here, you cannot sign the form. If you sign the form, you've decided you don't believe what's in here." He turned the chapbook over once in his hands. "I signed. I've carried this twenty-six years. I considered burning it — many times. But burning the last copy in this district is a different kind of signing."

He put it back in his pocket.

Corin thought about the singing girl — the funeral-comfort song, the melody that had sounded like grief repurposed. He had not known, at the time, that he was hearing a dead boy's message to the future. He had understood the song without knowing the words. He wondered, now, whether that was what Deyan had intended — a transmission built to be felt rather than decoded, so that even the people who could not read the frequency map were still, somehow, carrying it forward.

"The fifth apprentice," Theron continued. "Isara Fell. She was the youngest — sixteen. Her Resonance sensitivity was in the ninety-eighth percentile. Higher than yours. Her Echo didn't show the past. It showed something that hadn't happened yet — a presence in the deep Resonance layers, communicating in harmonic pulses. She called it 'a heartbeat that isn't human, isn't animal, isn't anything we have a word for.' She was recalibrated at double the standard intensity. The Archive classified her report and sealed her autopsy. She was twenty-two when she died."

"What was down there?"

Theron was silent for a long moment. In the distance, someone was hammering — the rhythmic sound of metal on metal, someone repairing something that had broken and could be fixed.

"I don't know. But the Archive was terrified of it. Six years ago, Arcwright authorized a deep Resonance expedition — classified at the highest level. They detected a structured signal originating from below the mapped layers. Repeating. Increasing in amplitude. The expedition report was sealed by Council order. Whatever Isara saw at sixteen, the Archive has been tracking it ever since — and whatever it is, it's getting closer."

Corin absorbed this in silence. A heartbeat that wasn't human, wasn't animal, wasn't anything we have a word for. He thought about his father's anchor room — the pre-collapse office embedded inside the Resonance, stable while everything around it destabilized. He thought about the Boundary Dissolution Theory that the research notes had mentioned, the heretical idea that the boundary between a person and their timeline was an illusion. If the Resonance was not a passive substrate but an active consciousness — something that had been present long enough to learn to communicate — then the anchor was not just a survival mechanism. It was an address. A marker that said: *I am here. I am listening. I want to talk.*

His father had built a way to survive. He might also have built a way to introduce himself to whatever lived in the deep.

"What about the station walk?" Corin asked.

Theron's expression shifted — not surprise exactly, but something adjacent to it. "How do you know about that?"

"Something I overheard in Harrow. A phrase that didn't make sense at the time. 'The station walk.' I thought it was a metaphor."

"It's not." Theron leaned forward, his voice dropping. "The Stations of Unwriting. It's a ritual the Senior Archivists perform when an entire timeline is erased. Nine fixed phases, each corresponding to a specific Resonance frequency. The erasing Chronometrist walks through a spiral corridor in the Citadel — nine chambers, each tuned to its station's frequency. The ritual is supposed to prevent the Chronometrist from developing anomalous Echoes. It doesn't always work. Lian Asher walked those chambers as an observer during her sensitivity training. She saw an erasure in progress — a public square, a speech about Timelost detainees, an entire timeline being unwritten while she watched. That was her anomalous Echo. That was what the calibration was supposed to erase."

"The station walk — where is it?"

"Below the decommissioned eastern sector. The spiral corridor connects the calibration annex to the deep Archive vaults. If you're going through the eastern sector, you'll pass directly above it. You won't be able to access it — the entrance requires Senior clearance — but you'll feel it. The Resonance bleed from the lower chambers is distinct. It feels like... pressure. Like something vast is pressing against the other side of reality, waiting to be let through."

Corin stood. The sun was lower now, the shadows longer, the amber light deepening toward dusk.

He was wearing maintenance technician's clothes — Iris had found them from a camp clothing exchange, a gray uniform that had been worn by enough different people over the years to carry no single identity. The forged credentials from Wynn were in his front pocket. The data chip with the patrol gaps was in the lining of his boot, sealed behind a strip of adhesive that would not pass a physical search but would survive incidental motion. The data crystal from his father's anchor room was in his chest pocket, next to the photograph. He had checked these items three times since returning from the waystation. This was the fourth time he was cataloguing them, not because he had forgotten anything but because the act of accounting was a way of staying present inside a body that wanted very badly to follow the implications of what Theron had just told him somewhere far from any action it was possible to take in the next four hours.

Nine chambers in a spiral descent. Lian Asher watching an entire timeline being unwritten. Deyan Koss encoding the evidence in a melody that still circulated sixty years later, in a language no one could speak anymore. Isara Fell detecting something that had not, technically, happened yet — and being killed for it, and that something still increasing in amplitude, still getting closer, still transmitting its structured signal into a Resonance that was dying from the same erasures that had killed the apprentices who first tried to describe it.

He had a ventilation duct to find, a terminal to reach, and a Resonance bleed to navigate without accessing the one sense the Archive had trained him to rely on.

"One more thing," Theron said. "When you reach the decommissioned annex, look for a terminal with amber indicator lights. The standard terminals use blue. The amber ones are connected to the old Reckoner network — the network Sable was part of before she was deprecated. If she's kept the routing active, that terminal is your way back to her."

Corin nodded. He started to leave, then stopped.

"Deyan Koss. Isara Fell. The third apprentice — the one who saw the Archive-Unwoven negotiation. And the fourth — male, age seventeen, nothing unusual in his file except that he survived five calibrations before the sixth killed him. What were their names?"

Theron looked up. His eyes were tired and still. "The third apprentice was Mirin Voss. She was eighteen. The fourth — I don't know his name. His file was purged before I could access it. I've been trying to reconstruct it for twenty-two years."

"If you find it — "

"I will tell you. I promise." Theron picked up his cup. The liquid inside had gone cold. "I have been waiting for someone to ask their names for two decades. You are the first."

Corin left the kitchen tent and walked toward the administration hub, and the weight of the dead was heavier than the data crystal in his pocket, heavier than the photograph of his father, heavier than the seventy-thousand-credit bounty that was already drawing tracker teams across the transitional zones. He carried them all — the five apprentices, the erased refugees, the deprecated Reckoner in the sub-basement, the cook who had signed the paper that killed a seventeen-year-old and had never forgiven himself for it. He carried them because no one else would, and because the alternative was letting them stay buried in sealed files and classified autopsy reports, and because some debts could only be paid by remembering what the debtor wanted forgotten.

The night was coming. The patrol gaps on Wynn's data chip would open in four hours. The ventilation duct would be waiting in the dark below the Citadel's eastern wall, exactly where a man who had once been an Archivist said it would be, and Corin Draven would crawl through it toward a terminal with amber lights and a Reckoner who had promised to witness his father's death and a truth that had been buried in sealed records for thirty years.

He told himself he was ready. The word felt like a borrowed coat — the right shape, not quite the right fit. Ready was what the Archive's instructors told apprentices before certification exams. Ready was a posture, a presentation, a claim made to the people watching that the person in front of them had internalized the material and could be trusted to apply it under pressure. What Corin felt was something different and more difficult to name: not confidence, exactly, but the specific clarity that followed from knowing there was no alternative route. He was going in through the duct because the duct was the way. He was going tonight because tonight was the window. He was carrying the names of five dead apprentices and the photograph of a father who was dying faster now, and none of that changed what he was about to do. It just changed what he understood it to mean.

chapters.title: Chapter 10: The Infiltration
chapters.text:
The ventilation duct was narrower than Theron had described.

Corin crawled through it on his stomach, his shoulders scraping against corroded metal, the darkness so complete that his eyes had stopped trying to adjust and had instead started inventing shapes — walls that were not there, turns that did not exist, the faces of people he had met and lost already assembling themselves in the void. He counted his body lengths: seventeen, eighteen, twenty-two. The duct had a slight downward grade, barely perceptible, the kind of angle that was designed to drain condensation rather than direct traffic. The metal wept where temperature differentials had worked moisture into the seams — cold beads against the backs of his hands, the specific discomfort of something unpleasant and unavoidable and genuinely irrelevant to anything that mattered tonight.

At body-length twenty-eight, the duct branched. Theron had not mentioned a branch. Corin stopped, pressed his palm flat against the metal floor, and listened. Both passages carried the same faint ozone edge, but the left branch had something else beneath it — a lower frequency, a pressure change he could not name, the specific quality of air that had been contained near an active Resonance source for a very long time. He was not accessing the Resonance. He was reading it the way a person reads a room through the sound of their own breathing in it. He took the right branch. The duct narrowed another five centimeters. His elbows were going to bleed tomorrow, assuming there was a tomorrow, assuming the bleeding mattered at all relative to the alternative outcomes currently distributed across the probability space of the next four hours. He stopped cataloguing the alternatives and crawled.

The air tasted of rust and old dust and something sharper underneath — the ozone tang of Resonance bleed, faint but present, a frequency just below the threshold of conscious perception that pressed against the inside of his skull the way a sound heard through walls pressed against the room that contained you. He could not access the Resonance. He could not tune. He was blind in the one sense the Archive had spent three years teaching him to trust, and the blindness was not terrifying but disorienting — like navigating a room he knew by sight using only touch, the map still present but the reading methodology changed. He had always assumed that the Resonance was a tool his training had provided him. He was beginning to understand that his training had also made it a dependency. The duct was teaching him what he could do without it.

The duct opened into a corridor.

The corridor was dark except for a single amber strip light flickering at the far end, its glow casting long shadows across walls that had not been cleaned in eleven years. The air was thicker here — the ozone tang stronger, the Resonance bleed pressing against the edges of Corin's awareness like a sound heard through water. Theron had been right. The eastern sector was not merely abandoned. It was saturated. Every surface hummed at a frequency just below audibility, and the hum was not neutral — it carried something, a quality Corin could not name, a sensation that reminded him of the moment before his Echo shattered during the exam, the fraction of a second when the dying man's mouth shaped words he could not hear.

He had not accessed the Resonance in three days. The withdrawal was a physical ache — his body trained to the harmonic baseline, his nervous system calibrated to frequencies that were now denied to him. The ache was a reminder: every Chronometrist who had ever been cut off from the Resonance described it as phantom-limb pain, the sensation of reaching for something that was no longer there. Sable had told him about the apprentices — their calibrations, their deaths, the way their Resonance signatures faded from the monitoring grid one by one. The withdrawal was not physical damage. It was the Archive's leash. Stay inside the system and the system keeps you alive. Leave, and you learn what it costs.

He moved down the corridor.

The eastern sector had been a research wing before the calibration accident — long rooms with observation windows, equipment racks stripped of their contents, floor panels warped by the Resonance bleed that had killed three technicians and been classified as "contained" in the official report. The report was a lie. The bleed was not contained. It had spread through the sector's entire infrastructure, saturating the walls, the floor, the air itself. The Archive had sealed the wing not because the accident was resolved but because the cost of resolving it exceeded the cost of abandoning it. Eleven years of budget allocations and Council restructurings and "temporary" status reports, and the eastern sector had been forgotten — not erased, but left to decay at a frequency the Archive could not hear and did not want to.

Corin found the decommissioned calibration annex at the end of the third corridor.

The annex was smaller than the active one in the Citadel's main wing — a circular room with a Resonance array embedded in the floor, its copper inlays dull with corrosion, its central node dark. The control console was against the far wall, its screen cracked, its keyboard missing half its keys. A secondary terminal sat in the corner — a pre-collapse unit, older than the others, its casing scuffed and dented and held together with what appeared to be hand-spliced cables and yellowed insulating tape.

The indicator lights were amber.
